title: Special Senses Vocabulary
description: Essential terminology for vision, hearing, equilibrium, taste, and smell

questions.q: The photoreceptors responsible for color vision are:
questions.explain: Cones detect color and provide sharp central vision. Three types (red, green, blue) combine signals to create color perception.

optionExplains.text: Rods detect light/dark only — they provide night vision but no color.
Cones — concentrated in the fovea, they need bright light to function. Three cone types (S, M, L) respond to different wavelengths, enabling trichromatic color vision.
Bipolar cells relay signals from photoreceptors to ganglion cells — not photoreceptors themselves.
Ganglion cells send signals to the brain via the optic nerve — not photoreceptors.

questions.q: The fovea centralis is the region of:
options: Highest cone concentration and visual acuity
questions.explain: The fovea is a tiny pit in the macula where cones are densely packed. It provides the sharpest vision for reading and detailed tasks.

optionExplains.text: Rods are absent from the fovea — they're in the peripheral retina.
Highest cone concentration — the fovea contains only cones, tightly packed with 1:1 connections to ganglion cells. This gives maximum visual acuity for central vision.
Optic nerve exit is the optic disc — the blind spot, not the fovea.
Peripheral vision uses rods outside the fovea.

questions.q: The iris regulates the amount of light entering the eye by changing the size of the:
questions.explain: The iris is a muscular diaphragm that constricts or dilates the pupil — the central opening that admits light.

optionExplains.text: The lens changes shape for focusing, not light quantity.
Pupil — the iris contains circular (sphincter) and radial (dilator) muscles. Bright light triggers constriction; dim light triggers dilation. The pupil is just a hole.
Cornea is fixed in size — it refracts light but doesn't regulate quantity.
Retina receives light but doesn't control how much enters.

questions.q: The structure that changes shape to focus light on the retina is the:
questions.explain: The lens changes shape (accommodation) to focus light from objects at different distances onto the retina.

optionExplains.text: Cornea provides fixed refraction — most of the eye's focusing power but no accommodation.
Iris controls pupil size, not focusing.
The lens — ciliary muscle contraction changes lens shape. For near objects, the lens becomes more convex (rounder). With age, the lens stiffens (presbyopia).
Vitreous humor maintains eye shape but doesn't change for focusing.

questions.q: The cochlea is the structure responsible for:
questions.explain: The cochlea is a spiral tube in the inner ear containing the organ of Corti — where sound vibrations are converted to neural signals.

optionExplains.text: Balance involves vestibular apparatus — semicircular canals and otolith organs.
Hearing — the cochlea converts sound waves to nerve impulses. Hair cells in the organ of Corti bend in response to fluid waves, triggering action potentials.
Taste involves taste buds on the tongue.
Smell involves olfactory epithelium in the nasal cavity.

questions.q: The three small bones in the middle ear are called:
questions.explain: The ossicles — malleus, incus, and stapes — form a chain that transmits and amplifies sound from the eardrum to the oval window.

optionExplains.text: Semicircular canals are inner ear structures for rotational balance.
Ossicles — the smallest bones in the body. Malleus (hammer) attaches to eardrum, incus (anvil) connects them, stapes (stirrup) pushes on the oval window.
Otoliths are calcium carbonate crystals in vestibular organs.
Cochlear chambers are fluid-filled spaces, not bones.

questions.q: The semicircular canals detect:
questions.explain: Semicircular canals detect rotational (angular) acceleration — head turning in any plane. Three canals oriented at right angles cover all rotations.

optionExplains.text: Linear acceleration is detected by otolith organs (utricle and saccule).
Rotational acceleration — when you turn your head, fluid (endolymph) lags behind, bending hair cells in the ampulla. Each canal detects rotation in its plane.
Sound frequency is analyzed in the cochlea, not semicircular canals.
Light intensity is an eye function.

questions.q: The retina is part of which tunic of the eye?
questions.explain: The retina is the inner sensory tunic — containing photoreceptors and neural processing layers that detect light and begin visual processing.

optionExplains.text: Fibrous tunic is the outer layer — cornea and sclera.
Vascular tunic (uvea) is middle — choroid, ciliary body, iris.
Sensory/neural tunic — the retina is the innermost layer, containing rods, cones, bipolar cells, and ganglion cells. It's actually part of the brain developmentally.
Conjunctiva is a mucous membrane covering the sclera, not a tunic.

questions.q: The organ of Corti is located on the:
questions.explain: The organ of Corti sits on the basilar membrane in the cochlea. Hair cells project into the tectorial membrane above.

optionExplains.text: Vestibular membrane forms the roof of cochlear duct — no organ of Corti there.
Basilar membrane — the organ of Corti's base. When sound waves vibrate it, hair cells bend against the tectorial membrane, generating neural signals.
Tectorial membrane overlies the organ of Corti — hair cells touch it, but it's not the base.
Oval window is where stapes vibrations enter the cochlea — not where organ of Corti sits.

questions.q: Aqueous humor is produced by the:
questions.explain: The ciliary body produces aqueous humor, which fills the anterior and posterior chambers, nourishing the lens and cornea.

optionExplains.text: The lens is nourished BY aqueous humor — doesn't produce it.
Ciliary body — its processes secrete aqueous humor into the posterior chamber. It flows through the pupil to the anterior chamber, draining via the canal of Schlemm.
Cornea receives nutrients from aqueous humor — no production.
Retina is photoreceptor layer — unrelated to aqueous humor production.

questions.q: The eustachian tube connects the middle ear to the:
questions.explain: The eustachian (auditory) tube connects the middle ear to the nasopharynx, equalizing pressure across the eardrum.

optionExplains.text: Outer ear connects via the external auditory meatus, not eustachian tube.
Inner ear connects to middle ear via the oval and round windows.
Nasopharynx — the eustachian tube opens when you swallow or yawn, equalizing middle ear pressure with atmospheric pressure.
Sinuses don't directly connect to the middle ear.

questions.q: Rhodopsin is the photopigment found in:
questions.explain: Rhodopsin is the visual pigment in rods — extremely sensitive to dim light. It consists of opsin protein plus retinal (vitamin A derivative).

optionExplains.text: Cones have different photopsins (color opsins), not rhodopsin.
Rods contain rhodopsin — 'visual purple' that bleaches in light and regenerates in darkness. This is why you need time to adapt when entering a dark room.
Bipolar cells don't contain photopigments — they're relay neurons.
Ganglion cells project to the brain — no photopigments.

questions.q: The optic disc is also known as the:
questions.explain: The optic disc is where ganglion cell axons exit to form the optic nerve. No photoreceptors exist here — creating a blind spot.

optionExplains.text: Fovea is the area of sharpest vision — opposite of a blind spot.
Blind spot — the optic disc has no rods or cones because nerve fibers and blood vessels pass through here. Your brain fills in the gap so you don't notice it.
Macula is the central retinal area containing the fovea.
Ora serrata is the junction between retina and ciliary body.

questions.q: The utricle and saccule detect:
questions.explain: The utricle and saccule are otolith organs — they detect linear acceleration (starting/stopping) and head position relative to gravity.

optionExplains.text: Sound waves are detected by the cochlea.
Rotational movement is detected by semicircular canals.
Linear acceleration and head position — otoliths (calcium carbonate crystals) pull on hair cells when you accelerate linearly or tilt your head.
Light intensity is an eye function.

questions.q: Olfactory receptors are located in the:
questions.explain: Olfactory epithelium lines the roof of the nasal cavity. Its bipolar neurons have cilia that detect odor molecules and send signals directly to the olfactory bulb.

optionExplains.text: Tongue has taste receptors, not olfactory.
Nasal cavity roof — olfactory epithelium covers about 5 cm² on each side. Odor molecules dissolve in mucus and bind receptors on olfactory cilia.
Pharynx is the throat — no specialized olfactory receptors.
Larynx is the voice box — respiratory, not olfactory.

questions.q: The tympanic membrane is also called the:
questions.explain: The tympanic membrane (eardrum) vibrates in response to sound waves, transferring energy to the ossicles.

optionExplains.text: Oval window is where stapes contacts the inner ear.
Eardrum — a thin, cone-shaped membrane separating outer and middle ear. Sound waves cause it to vibrate, which moves the malleus attached to its inner surface.
Round window is a membrane below the oval window that dissipates cochlear pressure waves.
Vestibule is the central inner ear chamber — not a membrane.

questions.q: The five primary taste sensations are sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and:
questions.explain: Umami is the fifth basic taste — triggered by glutamate. It gives the savory, meaty flavor found in parmesan, soy sauce, and mushrooms.

optionExplains.text: Spicy is pain/heat sensation (capsaicin receptors), not taste.
Umami — Japanese for 'delicious taste.' Glutamate receptors detect this savory quality. It's why MSG enhances food flavor.
Metallic is a taste sensation but not one of the five primary categories.
Astringent is a mouth feel (puckering), not a basic taste.

questions.q: The choroid layer of the eye:
options: Contains blood vessels that nourish the retina
questions.explain: The choroid is the vascular middle tunic — its rich blood supply nourishes the outer retina, and its pigment absorbs stray light.

optionExplains.text: The choroid is pigmented, not transparent.
Blood vessels nourish the retina — the choroid is highly vascular. It supplies photoreceptors from the outside while retinal vessels supply inner layers.
Light refraction is cornea and lens function.
Aqueous humor production is ciliary body function.

questions.q: The sensory neurons for smell are:
questions.explain: Olfactory sensory neurons are bipolar — one process extends to the nasal cavity surface, the other sends axons to the olfactory bulb.

optionExplains.text: Multipolar neurons have multiple dendrites — olfactory neurons are bipolar.
Bipolar neurons — olfactory neurons are unusual sensory neurons. Their dendrite-end has cilia for odor detection; their axon passes through the cribriform plate to the olfactory bulb.
Unipolar (pseudounipolar) are typical sensory neurons in spinal ganglia — not olfactory.
Interneurons are in the CNS — olfactory neurons are primary sensory.

questions.q: The sclera is the:
options: White outer layer of the eye
questions.explain: The sclera is the tough, white outer coat of the eye — the 'white of the eye.' It protects and maintains eye shape.

optionExplains.text: Transparent front is the cornea — continuous with sclera but clear.
White outer layer — the sclera is dense fibrous connective tissue. Extraocular muscles attach to it, and it maintains the eyeball's spherical shape.
Light-sensitive layer is the retina — inner tunic.
Colored part is the iris — middle tunic.

questions.q: Hair cells in the ear are:
questions.explain: Hair cells are mechanoreceptors — they convert mechanical bending of their stereocilia into electrical signals. They're not neurons themselves.

optionExplains.text: Hair cells are receptor cells but not neurons — they synapse onto neurons.
Mechanoreceptors — hair cells transduce mechanical movement (sound waves, head rotation, acceleration) into neural signals. Their stereocilia bending opens ion channels.
Photoreceptors detect light — found in the retina, not ear.
Chemoreceptors detect chemicals — taste and smell.

questions.q: The vitreous humor fills the:
questions.explain: The vitreous chamber (cavity) is the large space behind the lens, filled with vitreous humor — a gel-like substance maintaining eye shape.

optionExplains.text: Anterior chamber (between cornea and iris) contains aqueous humor.
Posterior chamber (between iris and lens) also contains aqueous humor.
Vitreous chamber — the largest eye compartment. Vitreous humor is 99% water gel that presses the retina against the choroid and refracts light.
Middle ear contains air, not vitreous humor.

questions.q: Taste buds are located on projections called:
questions.explain: Papillae are the bumps on the tongue surface. Three types (circumvallate, fungiform, foliate) contain taste buds; filiform papillae do not.

optionExplains.text: Villi are intestinal projections for absorption.
Papillae — raised structures on the tongue. Taste buds nest in their walls, with taste pores opening to receive dissolved chemicals.
Cilia are motile projections — taste receptor cells have microvilli, not cilia.
Microvilli are ON taste receptor cells, but the overall structures are papillae.

questions.q: The nerve that carries visual information to the brain is the:
questions.explain: The optic nerve (CN II) carries retinal ganglion cell axons from the eye to the brain — specifically to the lateral geniculate nucleus and visual cortex.

optionExplains.text: Oculomotor (CN III) controls eye muscles — motor, not sensory.
Trigeminal (CN V) carries face sensation — not vision.
Optic nerve — about 1 million axons from retinal ganglion cells form this cranial nerve. It's actually a brain tract developmentally.
Facial (CN VII) controls facial muscles and carries taste — not vision.

questions.q: The part of the ear that collects sound waves is the:
questions.explain: The auricle (pinna) is the external ear flap that funnels sound waves into the ear canal toward the eardrum.

optionExplains.text: Cochlea is the inner ear structure for hearing — doesn't collect sound.
Auricle (pinna) — the visible external ear. Its shape helps localize sound sources and channels sound waves into the external auditory canal.
Stapes is a middle ear ossicle — transmits sound, doesn't collect it.
Vestibule is an inner ear chamber for balance, not sound collection.
